entry.title: NY Times: 22 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Zelensky Says Military Aid Could Free Up Exports, Easing Food Prices

  • Lucy Calkins Retreats on Phonics in Fight Over Reading Curriculum
  • Two Killed, Dozens Injured in Rare Northern Michigan Tornado

  • She Was Told Surgery Would Cost About $1,300. Then the Bill Came: $229,000.
  • Biden to Sign Ukraine Aid Bill During His Trip to Asia
  • Summer Lee, a Progressive Democrat, Wins House Primary in Pennsylvania
  • A Trooper’s Lethal Force Was Justified by His Boss’s Wife: The Prosecutor

  • Staycation, Again?

Envahissez Haïti, exhorte Wall Street. Les États-Unis s’exécutent.

Articles in this issue:

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 22 May, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 22 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-22 06:08AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Hong Kong should reopen to foreign countries first, chief of leading business chamber in city urges government
  • Hong Kong driver killed in early-morning collision with stationary tour bus
  • How Hong Kong’s next chief executive can show he means business with ‘result-oriented’ reform
  • Canada moves up to No 2 for Hong Kong students going abroad, with easy migration pathways a major draw
  • Hong Kong’s buyers give their collective cold shoulder to flats at 3 locations, buying only 15 per cent of 200 units on offer
  • If you thought Hong Kong was crawling with spiders, turns out you’re right: first-of-its-kind study catalogues 100 species
  • Hong Kong police say officer pointed pepper solution launcher, not gun, at suspect after video of stand-off goes viral
  • Top advisers to Hong Kong’s leader express shock over proposal senior civil servants receive record 7.26 per cent pay rise
  • Mali’s rosewood crisis ‘not happening in a vacuum’ – and China is a key buyer
  • Shanghai’s coronavirus cases fall to the lowest since March 20 as city keeps community infections at zero
  • China’s Big Tech sees new wave of job cuts as Covid lockdowns hurt economy
  • Taiwan and Shanghai – 2 different takes on tackling Covid-19
  • China-US relations: as world watches Ukraine, navy missions in the Pacific quietly raise tension
  • ‘Fat’ wedding: Chinese woman told to apologise for comments about photography studio she claimed ruined her bridal photos
  • Ukraine war lessons spur China military shipbuilding surge: experts
  • China-Russia relations: Chinese producers lament loss of once-lucrative Russian deals
  • Beijing orders millions more to work from home in targeted Covid containment drive
  • Russia’s war on Ukraine based on flawed logic, Chinese military scholar wrote in article scrubbed from the web
  • China history: abandoned ‘haunted house’ restored to former East-meets-West architectural glory
  • As Sri Lanka defaults for the first time, what’s next for the Rajapaksa family?
  • Biden says US ‘ready for anything North Korea does’ as he wraps up Seoul trip with Hyundai’s US$10 billion investment pledge
  • In South Asia, Bangladesh’s ‘deft’ balancing of US, China and India ties stands out
  • Indonesian-Chinese writer Marga T on guilt of having been born a girl: ‘it gave me cancer’
  • Coronavirus: North Korea reports ‘positive trend’ in Covid fight as ‘fever’ cases dip
  • Australia’s next prime minister came from humble beginnings, says ‘reach for the stars’
  • Two million stranded as worst floods in decades hit Bangladesh’s northeast
  • Australia election: Labor seizes power, PM-elect Anthony Albanese vows to unite country
  • Head of Myanmar’s military government begins peace talks with ethnic militia groups, but doubts remain
  • Biden touts wider regional cooperation in meeting with South Korea’s Yoon Suk-yeol, avoids direct mention of China
  • As Ranveer Brar of ‘Modern Love Mumbai’ trains Uttar Pradesh jail cooks, here’s what you need to know about India’s prison entrepreneurs
  • Japanese, Western officials walk out of Apec trade meet in protest over Russia’s invasion of Ukraine
  • ‘Puncture-proof’ airless tyres edge closer to becoming a reality
  • US baby milk formula airlift due to begin with flight from Europe; emergency programme
  • Tornado hits US’ northern Michigan, at least two dead
  • Moscow bans 900 prominent Americans from entering Russia, including Hollywood actor Morgan Freeman
  • Dangerous DIY baby milk formula recipes in US go viral as parents get desperate
  • Coronavirus: US faces ‘dire’ risk of vaccine rationing without more funds
  • Ukraine war: Turkey’s Erdogan talks to Nato hopefuls Sweden and Finland, urges an end to support for ‘terror’ groups
  • Ukraine war: Biden signs US$40 billion bill for Ukraine assistance during Asia trip
  • Who were Mariupol’s last defenders?
  • Trump says ‘suburban women’ could punish him for the overturning of Roe v Wade, hurting his 2024 re-election chances, according to Rolling Stone
  • ‘Breaking point’: soaring inflation drives cuts to hunger-relief programmes
  • Hillary Clinton approved leaking Trump-Russia link to media, court hears
  • Binance readies licensed cryptocurrency platform in Middle East in a bid to regain legitimacy
  • Climate change: More Chinese and Asian firms disclose greenhouse-gas emissions, but lag in reduction commitments, data quality
  • ‘Spectacular’ Louis Vuitton fashion show, attended by Eileen Gu, Gemma Chan and Maude Apatow, proves why digital events can’t compare to in-person experiences
  • Classic Hong Kong cinema: John Woo on action, violence, influences, growing up in poverty and working in Hollywood in 1997 interview
  • Why dating as an Asian-American woman can mean hyper-sexualisation and fetishisation, reducing people to objects
  • Cannes 2022: Three Thousand Years of Longing movie review – George Miller’s first feature since Mad Max: Fury Road is enchanting
  • 9 best new luxury hotels in Asia: Joali gets its second Maldives spot, The Standard opens in Bangkok, and Regent Hong Kong returns to Tsim Sha Tsui harbourfront
  • Meet Heidi Klum’s teen model daughter, Leni: she’s the face of Dior Beauty and Michael Kors, and even landed a Vogue Germany cover – yet still worked a summer job at an ice cream parlour
  • Inside Paris Hilton’s extravagant luxury car collection, from her ‘Paris-ized’ holographic BMW i8 Roadster and Barbie pink Bentley Continental GT, to the Mercedes-Benz her millionaire ex gave her
  • How does Neymar spend his millions? The Paris Saint-Germain footballer bought Bored Ape NFTs, reportedly flies in a Cessna 680 jet and splurged US$180,000 on 16 GaGá Milano watches and accessories
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: Economist: 21 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Politics
  • Business
  • KAL’s cartoon
  • War and farming: The food catastrophe
  • Sweden, Finland and NATO: The meaning of membership
  • Cryptocurrencies: Sink or swim
  • Immigration: State of disarray
  • Travel after covid-19: From asterisks to spiderwebs
  • Letters: On abortion, Jaroslav Hasek, doctors, Celtic, staff meetings
  • Finance and economics: Claudia Sahm on what is driving inflation in America
  • Finance and economics: Angel Ubide expects inflation to subside if supply shocks fade
  • Finance and economics: Jón Steinsson believes that a painless disinflation is no longer plausible
  • The food crisis: After the pestilence, after the war…
  • Benefits for immigrants: The welfare states
  • Domestic terrorism: Sickening theory and lethal practice
  • Supply chains: O’Hareport
  • Pennsylvania: Keystone-cop politics

  • Myanmar’s civil war (2): Pass the Kool-Aid

  • Chaguan: It’s all political

  • Free speech in Ethiopia: Gagged
  • Lebanon: Shake-up and stasis

  • Ukraine: The relief of Kharkiv
  • France’s new prime minister: First Borne
  • Germany: Another blow for Mr Scholz
  • Greece: Hemlock for hacks
  • Charlemagne: Giving faces to “faceless Eurocrats”
  • The future of transport: The road not taken
  • The Northern Ireland protocol: Here we go again
  • Police recruitment: The beat has changed
  • The cost of living: Get with the program
  • The NHS: Silent as the grave
  • A gas price that is going down: Wholesale gas prices in Britain have collapsed
  • The right to roam: Forgive us our trespasses
  • Bagehot: UKSA! UKSA! UKSA!
  • The propaganda front: The Putin Show
  • SoftBank: Hard landing
  • Elon Musk and Twitter: Trick or tweet?
  • Indian business: A new foundation
  • Stockmarket listings: Where did the cash go?
  • Retailing: Supermarket crash
  • Bartleby: Making brainstorming better
  • Schumpeter: Slow and unsteady
  • The world economy: Recession watch
  • China’s economy: When bad data are good
  • Inflation in America: Powder keg
  • Cryptocurrencies: Unstablecoin
  • Climate finance: Carbon sinks
  • Buttonwood: Is China uninvestible?
  • Dodgy data: Half-measures
  • Free exchange: Immaterial needs
  • Carbon nanotechnology: Pouring graphene’s bright future
  • Therapeutic art: Comic relief
  • Mankind and other animals: Arrival of the interspecies
  • Scoundrels of yore: Pleasure without principle
  • Ecofiction: Back to earth
  • Royalists and Roundheads: The dogs of war
  • Johnson: Enter the dragon app
  • Indicators: Economic data, commodities and markets
  • Climate change: That non-sinking feeling
  • Saotome Katsumoto: The forgotten fire
Global news and current affairs from a European perspective. Best downloaded on Friday mornings (GMT)

entry.title: The Diplomat: 21 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 20 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-20 10:56AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Northern India caught in vicious cycle of heatwaves and forest fires

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: Nature: 19 May, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The toll of menopause: how universities can help
  • Nature journals raise the bar on sex and gender reporting in research
  • How climate law can help to prevent the next pandemic
  • The metrics that predict the course of type 2 diabetes
  • Mysterious cosmic thief is stealing a dwarf galaxy’s gas

  • ‘Barcodes’ reveal a cancer’s life story

  • Flower power: ‘Sunflower’ system churns out useful energy
  • Helium levels in the atmosphere are ballooning

  • Rise of drug-resistant tuberculosis is hidden in plain sight
  • AI can burn or brighten, and intangible economics: Books in brief

  • One statistical analysis must not rule them all
  • Childcare crowdfunding campaigns aim to keep mums on the academic track
  • Immune to frustrations
  • Giant collision created galaxies devoid of dark matter
  • Young cerebrospinal fluid improves memory in old mice
  • A lab-on-a-chip that takes the chip out of the lab
  • Killing the messenger to evade bacterial defences
  • Tension hones body segmentation around the clock
  • A trail of dark-matter-free galaxies from a bullet-dwarf collision
  • Direct observation of the dead-cone effect in quantum chromodynamics
  • Polariton Bose–Einstein condensate from a bound state in the continuum
  • Observation of a molecular bond between ions and Rydberg atoms
  • Microcomb-driven silicon photonic systems
  • Microfluidic chain reaction of structurally programmed capillary flow events
  • Programmable heating and quenching for efficient thermochemical synthesis
  • Intermolecular [2π+2σ]-photocycloaddition enabled by triplet energy transfer
  • Synergistic HNO3–H2SO4–NH3 upper tropospheric particle formation
  • Expanding ocean food production under climate change
  • A natural mutator allele shapes mutation spectrum variation in mice
  • Genetic and chemotherapeutic influences on germline hypermutation
  • Young CSF restores oligodendrogenesis and memory in aged mice via Fgf17
  • Left–right symmetry of zebrafish embryos requires somite surface tension
  • Phage anti-CBASS and anti-Pycsar nucleases subvert bacterial immunity
  • Group A Streptococcus induces GSDMA-dependent pyroptosis in keratinocytes
  • Landscape of helper and regulatory antitumour CD4+ T cells in melanoma
  • Selective inhibition of miRNA processing by a herpesvirus-encoded miRNA
  • Nonlinear mechanics of human mitotic chromosomes
  • Design of protein-binding proteins from the target structure alone
  • Structural basis of NPR1 in activating plant immunity
  • USP14-regulated allostery of the human proteasome by time-resolved cryo-EM
  • Author Correction: The guidance receptor plexin D1 is a mechanosensor in endothelial cells
  • Publisher Correction: Signatures of TOP1 transcription-associated mutagenesis in cancer and germline
  • Addendum: Large metallicity variations in the Galactic interstellar medium
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 20 May, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 20 May, 2022 published at 2022-05-20 00:16AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “The Bangalore Detectives Club” by Harini Nagendra
  • Podcast with Glynne Walley, translator of “Eight Dogs, or ‘Hakkenden’: Part One—An Ill-Considered Jest” by Kyokutei Bakin
  • New Book Announcement: “Stone Masters: Power Encounters in Mainland Southeast Asia”, edited by Holly High
  • “Vernacular English: Reading the Anglophone in Postcolonial India” by Akshya Saxena
  • “Breathless” by Amy McCulloch
  • Eliot and I
  • “Everest 1922: The Epic Story of the First Attempt on the World’s Highest Mountain” by Mick Conefrey
  • New Book Announcement: “Still Lives” by Reshma Ruia
  • “All the Lovers in the Night” by Mieko Kawakami
  • “Letters to Singapore” by Kelly Kaur
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

  • Rosa Lyster - Empire of Diamonds: Victorian Gems in Imperial Settings  / Blood, Sweat and Earth: The Struggle for Control over the World’s Diamonds
  • Jan-Werner Müller - Germany Inc.
  • A.E. Stallings - Poem: ‘The Sieve’
  • Ferdinand Mount - Promised Lands: The British and the Ottoman Middle East
  • James Romm - Olympia: A Cultural History
  • Ange Mlinko - Rapture and Melancholy: The Diaries of Edna St Vincent Millay
  • Tim Parks - Kapo
  • Hal Foster - At Tate Modern: ‘Surrealism beyond Borders’
  • Freya Johnston - The Life of the Mind
  • Alex Harvey - The N’Gustro Affair  / No Room at the Morgue
  • Anne Enright - On the Sands
  • Francis Gooding - Wild Thought: A New Translation of ‘La Pensée sauvage’
  • Rosemary Hill - Diary: At Mars Avenue
Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
